summary: Gruber marvels at Apple's stock trajectory, noting the company has reached a $120 billion market cap — nearly rivaling HP and Google, and closing the gap on Microsoft. He contrasts this with five years earlier, when Microsoft's market cap was 33 times Apple's. He then discloses that he owns no Apple stock and no stock in any company he covers, establishing a personal ethics policy modeled on traditional journalism standards. He argues this transparency is essential for reader trust.
key_points: Apple's market cap grew from $6.3 billion to $120 billion in five years, closing the gap with Microsoft dramatically
Gruber publicly discloses he owns no Apple stock or stock in any company he covers, establishing a journalistic conflict-of-interest policy
He argues that blogs can move stock prices, citing Engadget's false iPhone delay report as evidence
He models his disclosure policy on traditional newspaper and magazine standards for financial conflicts of interest
money_quotes: Five years ago, Microsoft's market cap was about 33 times greater than Apple's — $210 billion to $6.3 billion.
Needless to say, this is an interesting trend.
I do not own a single share of Apple Inc.; nor do I own stock in any other individual company I might write about on Daring Fireball.
Good newspapers and magazines prohibit their reporters from owning stock in the companies they write about, and for good reason.

I think it's worth stating this as policy so that you, my readers, can trust that what I write — as well as what I know about but choose not to write — is based solely on what I deem interesting, important, and true.
key_insight: As Apple's stock surge made financial conflicts increasingly relevant for tech commentators, Gruber drew a line between blogging and journalism by voluntarily adopting traditional media's conflict-of-interest standards.
